column13:
I'm writing in response to your request for pedestrian safety data on the CT Open Data Portal. The Department of Transportation shared two datasets that have information that might be useful to you:

Shoulders: https://connecticut-ctdot.opendata.arcgis.com/datasets/CTDOT::ctdot-roadway-classification-and-characteristic-data?layer=25
The ShoulderUsageDescription column includes "Bike" as an option
Lanes: https://connecticut-ctdot.opendata.arcgis.com/datasets/CTDOT::ctdot-roadway-classification-and-characteristic-data?layer=21 
The LaneTypeDescription column includes "Bike Only" as an option

These two datasets are vetted and maintained by DOT. DOT is also working on additional projects such as sidewalk collection and bike lanes, but those data are not yet publicly available. To access the data that are not yet public, you could submit a FOIA request to DOT. More information about FOIA is available here: https://portal.ct.gov/dot/general/foi-requests#:~:text=Making%20a%20Freedom%20Of%20Information%20(FOI)%20Request%20Through%20CTDOT,-Overview&text=The%20Connecticut%20Freedom%20of%20Information,online%20FOI%20request%20form%20here.&text=CTDOT%20has%20implemented%20an%20online%20FOI%20management%20system%20called%20GovQA..

column2: From the 2023 LiDAR data set, would the GIS office be able to make a roof centerline dataset? The COGs may lack the processing power to run a model to create this type of data. The background for this is during the CT GIS Network's Summer Webinar https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DVU938AB6Yk WestCOG presented a project on solar adoption and one data point they used was road orientation, assuming roofs generally follow the same centerline. I questioned the methodology, and the conclusion was there wasn't a dataset out there to validate the assumption, and the computing power needed to make such a dataset was outside the ability and scope of the project.
column7: For research (e.g. to find data for academic or public policy research)
column9: The original concept was validating theories about rooftop solar, but perhaps it can be used in other environmental projects like  evaluating whether roof orientation affects heat islands, or power consumption, or whether houses are built at an orientation that maximizes passive heat generation in the winter and cooling in the summer.

column2:
Dear Connecticut Research Team,
I hope you are doing well.  I conduct research with Dr. Friedman and the TEMPO Lab at the Yale School of Public Health. We are currently researching recreational cannabis policies across the United States. More specifically, we are looking at the opt-out decisions by municipality.

Based on our reading of the Uniform Controlled Substances Act, we understand that Connecticut is required to collect information regarding each municipality’s choice. Is there a central repository for which you collect opt-outs/or information by municipality for the time-frame 2018-2024 with us?

Your help will enable us to accurately analyze Connecticut's cannabis landscape – ensuring CT is accurately represented in the project we are constructing for public use.
column7: For research (e.g. to find data for academic or public policy research)
column9:
CAPS Project Specifics Summary, Scope, Related Projects, and CollaboratorsProject Summary:
This project seeks to understand the impact of state and local cannabis policies on tobacco and nicotine product use. Researchers will collect data on a wide array of cannabis and tobacco policies, and analyze how these shape cannabis access as well as youth and adult use of cigarettes, cigars, e-cigarettes, other nicotine products, and cannabis. 

PI and Co-investigators:
Dr. Abigail Friedman at Yale University (PI)
Dr. Rosalie Pacula at University of Southern California (USC):
Dr. Michael Pesko at University of Missouri (Mizzou):
Dr. Jennifer Pearson at University of Nevada, Reno (UNR):
Dr. Sarah Stith at University of New Mexico (UNM):

column9: Use for genealogy. The use of 3rd party companies to index data is a conflict of interest as it is typically behind a paywall (such as "Ancestry.com"). Driving around Connecticut to compile the data is time consuming, requires physical access in many cases to the records (even if they are digitized) unless a premium is paid for digital access (which does not guarantee availability). This type of research does not require a physical copy of the record, just the data itself.

column2:
I am a PhD student from Brown University, and I am working on my thesis about caregiver support and eldercare. To understand who are using the state Paid Family and Medical Leave (PFML) program to care for older adults with serious illness, I would like to request data on PFML claims (especially for caring spouse and parents) from 2021 to 2024, specifically: 

1.	Number of claims approved for caregiving, and caregiving for child, spouse, parents, and all other family members separately, by year 
2.	Total amount of benefits ($) paid for caregiving claims, and for caregiving claims for child, spouse, parents, and all other family members separately, by year 
3.	The average number of weeks approved for caregiving claims, and caregiving claims for child, spouse, parents, and all other family members separately, by year 

If you have any of the demographic information, I would like the above three tables to be further broken down by sex, age, race, income, or firm size of applicants.
column7: For research (e.g. to find data
column9: I plan to write a paper about who are using the paid family and medical leave benefits, how the benefits are used, and whether the utilization is associated with sociodemographic characteristics of applicants or associated with the benefit designs. I sincerely appreciate your time and please let me know if you need any clarifications.
column13: I'm writing in response to your request for data on PFML claims. Please contact the CT Paid Leave Authority directly with your request. John Simonetti is the person to contact for your request, and you can reach him at john.simonetti@ct.gov.

column2:
Hi,

I am a PhD student at the University of Pittsburgh. Our research lab focuses on labor market economics and the future of work. As part of our research, we are analyzing unemployment trends across different industries, time periods, and counties. 

Therefore, I would like to obtain the historical unemployment claim data broken down by county and by industry (ideally using NAICS codes) �  I noticed that the website offers claim data broken down by industry and claim data broken down by county (town), but it does not provide claim data broken down simultaneously by both county and industry.

I would appreciate your advice on where these series can be downloaded and whether a regularly updated API is available for automated long-term access.

column2: I am requesting the number of board-certified PAs whose primary specialty is Emergency Medicine, practicing in Connecticut.
column7: For research (e.g. to find data for academic or public policy research)
column9:
I am conducting an IRB-exempt research study examining the use of Point-of-Care Ultrasound (POCUS) among PAs in Connecticut Emergency Departments. 

The goal of this study is to better understand how PAs in emergency medicine use POCUS in clinical practice. Our aim is to address the current gap in the literature regarding POCUS use by PAs and to contribute to the development of evidence-based guidelines and policies to support POCUS education and implementation. 

To calculate an accurate survey response rate and describe the study population, I need an aggregate head-count of PAs credentialed specifically to emergency-department cost centers at CT hospitals.

Requested extract:
Metric: Number of PAs whose primary cost center is the Emergency Department
Breakout: Each acute-care hospital (or health-system) in Connecticut
Time frame: Most recent full calendar year (CY 2024) or latest reporting quarter
